item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I love the concept of an agent that wants to help an SCP, against the foundation's wishes, especially with the gender dysphoria spin. Though, I really don't think the ethics commitee would be so quick to allow an SCP to be decommisioned, considering the foundation's primary directive. I also don't think the agent/SCP would have made the connection so quickly, considering the dialogue. Overall, I think you could've convinced me of these things if only more time was spent developing them. If only!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I agree with a lot of your points, stormbreath. Though I do think the ideas are fine, I downvoted the article because of the execution of the ideas. For example, though I don't mind Hayes being trans, I don't like the execution of it.

I'm not sure what else to add since most or all of what I don't like about SCP-4175 has already been said by stormbreath, Legathar, and Shaggydredlocks. I'll add anything else if I think of any.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I upvoted this article in the past, but upon a reread it just doesn't work for me. I get what you're trying to do, but I don't think you are successful in actually accomplishing that well. From what I recall, I upvoted because I liked the intent enough, but on a reread, it's clear the execution of that intent isn't successful.

The first is that the mention of 113 is rather hamfisted way to establish that Hayes is trans. In universe, it feels out of place, and worse still, it's easy to gloss over and miss what you're going for. Aside from that one hint/mention, it's very easy to miss or just not get the idea that this is an allegory for gender dysphoria — which is very bad when that's the entire focus of the article! It could stand to be a lot more obvious with what's going on. You say that 113 is here to indicate something to the reader, but you could do that in dialogue — in fact, without it showing up noticeably in the dialogue, it feels like an informed attribute.

The story as a whole is also weak. You have an interview log where the idea the ghost suffers from dysphoria is established, Hayes brings up the idea of helping the ghost, and then it just … ends. If this a story about Hayes helping 4175 transition — as your comments here seem to imply — then you need to actually show that! You gloss over the actual story and cut straight from the introduction of the problem to the end! It's character-driven but the character development happens off-screen.

item.title: missing link
item.content:encoded: I think this page is missing the links to 4174 and 4176? i might've missed them tho

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
What you were aiming for went completely over my head on the first read-through, so much so that I questioned the seemingly random inclusion of the Series 1 crosslink.

Upon a second reading, it stand out even worse, as it's randomless becomes a very clear doylist element — there's no real reason for this to be mentioned from an in-universe perspective, it's just sitting clumsily atop the interview with no real relevancy… I can't see it as anything besides a giant sign that says "this is supposed to be a metaphor!".

I think Legathar's critique upthread hits the nail on the head — there is absolutely a more elegant way to get this across in the dialogue. You rob yourself of developing on this relationship and their common experiences by using a (seemingly) random crosslink carry the load of exposition instead of providing a more fulfilling character moment.

It doesn't read as if there's any underlying meaning… the closest the dialogue really gets to relating a trans experience:

+ show block
– hide block

SCP-4175: Both, I guess. You're alive and you have an awesome job, helping people. I think the worst part about being stuck in that mirror is that I know everyone outside is living their lives like everything is normal. I want to be like them so much.

ψ-8 Hayes: But you're trapped with yourself, which you hate the sight of?

SCP-4175: Yeah, actually.

ψ-8 Hayes: Well, I know a few tricks to stave off the existential dread. Want to hear them?

… is blink-and-you-miss-it. It doesn't at all feel like it's anything more than what it is on the surface, and Hayes reply at the end 'know a few tricks' can readily be interpreted as simply knowing how to help ghosties with coping mechanisms, because she's a ghostbuster.

Overall, I think there's a great seed here of both using the mirror aspect to convey disphoria, and linking passing over to transition, but it falls quite short of giving me a rewarding reading experience… I can't get behind a character piece that doesn't make me feel for the characters.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Great Job, and I hope to see more.

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
A little basic. I would've preferred to see more things that are currently implied being stated openly instead, just to flesh out the article a bit and let us see more of the interaction. For a big example, the video log was basically a dud. The ethics committee comment was good, even though I haven't read the rest of your canon.

I did like it enough for a +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Unfortunately I'm too hung up on the fact an agent was alone, with a potentially dangerous anomaly, for almost 50 minutes, and didn't get anything worse than a slap on the wrist. There's "cold not cruel", and there's just plain lapses in judgement.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I don't really like lgbt stories, but this one is clever.
+1

item.title: Re: Eidolons
item.content:encoded:
Strangely vehement, but alright.

+ show block
– hide block

still it tries to stand only on its gimmick and fails at that too.

What's my gimmick, exactly? You mean discussing trans stuff? I ask because you seem confused about the nature of the article when you make comments like:

since these bland characters only serve the ultime goal of making a pun

The cross-link to series one is out of place and unexplained.

If it's not trans stuff, what's my gimmick? Ghosts?

But anyway how can a professional slip up like this? You're talking to a dead kid? Can't you be more careful? More tactful?

It's a minor mistake on Maria's part. She was a tiny bit flustered because she's discussing intimate emotional issues with a stranger. It's not like she accidentally revealed he's been damned to hell for eternity.

State a motivation.

Maria's motivation is to help ghosts. Her motivation is slightly altered when she realizes she really connects to what Alex is going through. This is pretty clear from the dialogue, although I won't blame anyone for skimming it.

Even if it's to ease the nerves of the interviewee, why wouldn't one simply pretend to disconnect the recorder?

Maria doesn't want to lull the kid into a false sense of security. She genuinely wants to help, so she cuts the audio so the Foundation can't intrude on a very personal conversation.

Unless you had told us, I doubt anyone would have noticed he/she was even a kid.

I think it's pretty obvious from the dialogue. Again, I don't blame readers for skimming.

Yet you refuse to let your audience feel uncertainty or having their views challenged. You assure the audience that whatever they think the afterlife is, it is nice and fluffy, and that all you have to do is [REDACTED]. No tension, no build-up, no suspence.

That'd be a weird and awful story. The extended metaphor would read that post-transition life is equivalent to hell. Pretty sure being fully transitioned is a good thing; the issue is dealing with the emotional stuff on the way there.

Sorry you didn't like it, I guess. 🤷‍♀️

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This was really great. I think the comparison it describes is apt and I genuinely really liked Hayes. +1

item.title: Eidolons
item.content:encoded:
The article tries to be more character driven than plot-driven and fails, still it tries to stand only on its gimmick and fails at that too.

+ show block
– hide block

The idea isn't something we haven't seen already, done better already, but the worst part is it fails to utilize the format properly, opting instead to lazily use conventional storytelling. If you had explained clinically the conditions of the kid in the description, I doubt it would have been different, since these bland characters only serve the ultime goal of making a pun and have no will of their own whatsoever (see below).

Everything about this feels unprofessional and the dialogue is so artificial and akward it's almost cringey. The cross-link to series one is out of place and unexplained. The pauses don't help at all and if this was all deliberate, it doesn't seem to be the case at all.

(…) they'll probably call me in to exorcise you. Err, to help you to the afterlife.

Using “Err” seems a bit wrong, you could have used (filler) or (stutters) or anything else, really.
But anyway how can a professional slip up like this? You're talking to a dead kid? Can't you be more careful? More tactful? Was this a poor attempt at humour or implying that Psi-8 has little experience in actually helping ghosts and is instead used to exorcising them? Even so it's incredibly unprofessional and she faces no repercussions whatsoever for this.

Also does the exorcist casually breach the protocol everytime to “help” ghosts? No? Of course not. Then why was this time different?

State a motivation.
Is this her first time dealing with a child and she's nervous and doesn't know how to interact with one?
Is this her first time helping a ghost instead of exorcising it?
Is the term exorcise not negative at all and is colloquially used by Foundation personnel, but they refrain from using it with normies because it has bad connotations? (it literally means ''expelling an evil spirit'', how can this be?)

Do all Foundation operatives cut off the audio when discussing sensitive data of humanoid objects? No? Why did Wave Function Eight do it then?
They probably don't care about the privacy of normal people so why bother with the privacy of SCP objects?
Although I like the implication that dead people still have rights, it is implied that the Foundation already has a psychological profile for the skip and has done extensive research on its past. It's not violating privacy more than a dectetive violates the privacy of a victim. Even if it's to ease the nerves of the interviewee, why wouldn't one simply pretend to disconnect the recorder?

Again:
State a motivation.
Otherwise I'm going to assume you were just too lazy to come up with something. Personally I think that if you wanted to leave that part vague you should have simply used "[EXTRANEOUS DATA REDACTED]" and left some of the tips.
Did Psyeit do this to protect her own privacy? I doubt it, since she openly asked to use an SCP object for personal reasons revealing extremely sensitive data by doing so. (Political opinions, religious or philosophical beliefs, health, and sex life)

It's also unlikely that the omission of audio would stop the Foundation from reading their lips or atleast the lips of one of them and deducing the rest of the conversation through context.
Psate, a confident easygoing trans woman totally doesn't feel bad about herself anymore and is so confident in her confidence that she can help a dead kid deal with death (supposedly because of her condition) and I'm supposed to believe it without seeing it.

Not only she gets away with minimal punishment, she makes a request AND THEY APPROVE IT.

Alex Conrad is just a generic scared person. Genderless and ageless. Unless you had told us, I doubt anyone would have noticed he/she was even a kid. You attempt to "show and not tell", but you tell nothing when you show and show nothing when you tell.

Not only you're discussing a controversial aspect of the human psyche lazily, you also deal with existential dread, death and what comes after death. Yet you refuse to let your audience feel uncertainty or having their views challenged. You assure the audience that whatever they think the afterlife is, it is nice and fluffy, and that all you have to do is [REDACTED]. No tension, no build-up, no suspence.

Completely soulless. A pretty plate with no substance, shallow and bland. -1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This was a nice read. +1

item.title: Re: This scp.
item.content:encoded: I don't see the -J suffix. Alex is a deliberately vanilla ghost, because I don't care about his ghostiness for it's own sake. His reanimation is a vehicle to examine dysphoria, not a source of fear for the reader.

item.title: Re: SCPs aren't transgender blogs
item.content:encoded:
Not to mention that gendered SCPs have a long-standing history on the wiki.

Each of those words are a link btw

item.title: Re: SCPs aren't transgender blogs
item.content:encoded:
If the author wants to assign gender identity that's their call.

Funny thing, artistic freedom, you can do whatever you want with it.

item.title: Re: SCPs aren't transgender blogs
item.content:encoded: I agree, you know it’s weird to think that scps have genders unless they are humanoid or creatures that can actually mate, no scps don’t have genders I don’t know why an object can have a gender or have a sexuality, it makes no sense.

item.title: This scp.
item.content:encoded: This scp is very cheesy in ways, it’s like a 1999 horror ghost, is this a joke scp or supposed to be taken serious?

item.title: Re: SCPs aren't transgender blogs
item.content:encoded: authors can write what they want. That's like saying "you can't write about trans and cisgender stuff"

item.title: Re: Love the trans inclusion
item.content:encoded: Thanks, Phoebe! Happy you liked it.

item.title: Re: SCPs aren't transgender blogs
item.content:encoded:
What I DO care about, is the fact that literally the only point to this SCP is to drive home a vague but hamfisted metaphor about something that has no relevance to the SCP canon.

Accurate appraisal of my writing ability aside, the majority of SCPs have nothing to do with the central canon. This article is, however, intimately related to my Site-17 canon, so I don't know what you're mad about.

If I wanted to write an article about an army of trans catgirls breaking the masquerade, I could do it. The site has an infinite number of canons, and mine has trans and queer energy. That's no less valid than anything else.

item.title: Moderator Post
item.content:encoded:
I thought everyone had agreed last year that LGBT stuff would be kept off this site, but evidently not.

No one agreed that, and no one will be agreeing that in the future. The wiki is inclusive and accepting of everyone, and fiction can be written about anything or anyone.

This site is not for stories about gay and trans stuff, it's for stories about urban fantasy.

This site is for whatever form of writing the author decides it is for. The community ultimately decides what does and does not stay on the platform through the voting system, but there are no rules against LGBTQ+ related articles.

Please keep this in mind next time you post something to here.

You are not staff, and as such, cannot tell an author to keep in mind a fake rule.

There is nothing wrong with your critique specifically, but we do not support this kind of anti-LGBTQ+ speech on the wiki. Please do not do this again.

item.title: SCPs aren't transgender blogs
item.content:encoded:
I thought everyone had agreed last year that LGBT stuff would be kept off this site, but evidently not.

I'm don't care about transgenders. What I DO care about, is the fact that literally the only point to this SCP is to drive home a vague but hamfisted metaphor about something that has no relevance to the SCP canon. This site is not for stories about gay and trans stuff, it's for stories about urban fantasy. Please keep this in mind next time you post something to here.

-1

item.title: Love the trans inclusion
item.content:encoded:
trans rights!!!
great scp
sincerely: your local trans girl

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Didn’t really like it. I see how the rotting corpse is supposed to be like dysphoria, but I just thought that was an off hand remark to add to the horror of it, while the real horror was being stuck in the same small, dark room with nothing to do but stare at a world that goes on without you, but you can’t change. Also, the 113 reference seems kinda ham fisted(why would the foundation include one seemingly minor event in front of the interview?).

To see improve this, I suggest that you stop focusing my on the trapped in a mirror aspect, and instead focus on constantly staring at a horrifying version of yourself. Also having Hayes stop the audio half way through stops us from knowing what Hayes and 4175’s relationship is(same goes for the video log, why would the foundation ever turn the audio off?) I would also suggest that you move the 113 reference to the middle of the interview, maybe as an off hand reference from Hayes.

This has potential, but isn’t doing it for me as it is, so I’m gonna have to unfortunately downvote this. -1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: +1 for the fascinating concept, but if the kid got murdered, how did he not know he was already dead?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Thanks for the read! Knowledge of my weird subtextual story isn't necessary to understand this article, but Cimmerian makes what's going on in the background somewhat clear in his missive.

Taking into account past malfeasance relating to humanoid anomalies, failure to neutralize SCP-4175 will be seen as symptomatic of a misunderstanding of basic ethical practices persistent in Site-17 personnel, yourself included.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
huh, didn't really understand the pun until after reading this discussion, feel kinda dumb considering all the stuff that made it clear.

that said, i do really like this! but this seems to be a part of a series? im going to no-vote since im not super clear on where this takes place. its a cool skip otherwise, gj!

item.title: Technical Captain Post - Open
item.content:encoded:
Please don't add extraneous tags to your article. I've removed the inapplicable tags.

In the future wait for the tech team to tag the article if you're not sure how.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Same, it flew right over my head when I first read it. But I kinda like that; it feels subtle to me, so their interaction comes across as natural.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I absolutely missed the metaphor earlier, but now that I understand it better, I'm glad that you included it! Reading it again, I see the connection a lot better, and I'm bummed that I didn't catch it earlier!

+1 from me

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This made me smile. It's very nice, and Hayes, a trans female, helping a ghost transition (To the afterlife) is clever. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The parallels between the ghost's experiences and the experiences of transgender people are clever, and I can always appreciate a wholesome story.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: i really really liked this a lot. thanks for your work!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I'm inclined to agree. The original draft was far subtler, but it became evident during the critique process that a fair amount of readers wouldn't see the narrative extended beyond "sad ghost finds friend".

That being said, your criticism's entirely warranted, so I've removed the direct reference to helping ghosts transition in their dialogue. That should help a bit.

Happy you enjoyed!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I love the expansion on the Department of Spectral Phenomena, and ghost-busting in general, but the ties to gender dysphoria and such read fairly heavy-handedly to me. The parallel between being forced to stare at your own rotting corpse and being forced to see yourself in a body you don't identify with is a decently clever one (as far as I know; I'm not transgender, so I can't speak for how accurate it is), but would be much more clever if the interview didn't rub it in your face so much.

Ah well. The ending was wholesome enough, and I always enjoy Ethics Committee interactions, so… yeah. Tentative upvote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: She helped him transition… what a wholesome thematic pun.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Written for Pride Month 2019! 🏳️‍🌈

Hell hath no limits, nor is circumscribed in one self place. For where we are is hell, and where hell is must we ever be.

I got several question during the drafting process about the inclusion of SCP-113. Its inclusion serves to indicate to the reader that Hayes is transfemale, and that she has experiences with gender dysphoria. Given that SCP-4175's condition loosely parallels these experiences, she becomes inclined to help him transition to the afterlife.

Hope you all enjoyed! Special thanks to FloppyPhoenix and calamitous1 for providing feedback!
